exemplar.title: How Has The Concentration of PM2.5 Particles From 2004 to 2022 Influenced The Annual Mean Temperature in Jakarta

detailedExemplarData.title: How Has The Concentration of PM2.5 Particles From 2004 to 2022 Influenced The Annual Mean Temperature in Jakarta

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Consistent use of appropriate environmental terminology
Clear structure with a comprehensive Table of Contents
Logical flow of argument in most sections
weaknesses: Prose is occasionally verbose and repetitive
Formatting glitches and misplaced figures disrupt readability
Citation style is inconsistent and some in-text references are missing
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication spans band 2â3: organization and terminology are strong, and the argument is largely coherent. Despite verbosity and formatting issues, the submission meets topâband descriptors for structure and language clarity, justifying a 3/3.

strengths: Concise, focused research question specifying pollutant, timeframe, variable and location
Detailed local and global context with quantitative PM2.5 exceedance data and health impact statistics
Clear explanation of connections between air pollution, Urban Heat Island and temperature, supported by a contextual map
weaknesses: Minor stylistic issues (capitalisation, wordiness) in the aim statement
Abrupt ending to the Urban Heat Island mechanism discussion
Some contextual statistics lack explicit in-text citations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands of Identifying the Context meet band 5â6 descriptors: the research question is coherent and focused, the environmental issue is discussed in depth, and the links to the question are explained comprehensively. Minor wording and citation omissions prevent a top band score, so a 5/6 is appropriate.

strengths: Method is repeatable and appropriately addresses the correlation question
Equipment and variables tables are clear, supporting consistency
Sampling strategy and ethical considerations are described, showing awareness of bias mitigation
weaknesses: Does not address potential confounders or data homogeneity in sufficient depth
Sampling justification lacks statistical rigour regarding representativeness
Risk assessment omits licensing and privacy considerations for secondary data
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Planning components fall into band 3â4: the design is repeatable and sufficiently relevant, sampling and risks are described, but several methodological details and justifications are incomplete. The overall competence and clarity justify an upperâband score of 4/6.

strengths: Insightful evaluation linking findings to health impacts, UHI and policy
Well-structured identification of methodological strengths, weaknesses and limitations
Meaningful suggestions for modifications and further research
weaknesses: Figure references are inconsistent, causing reader confusion
Limitations evaluation lacks quantitative assessment of biases
Improvement suggestions lack detailed implementation plans
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Discussion and Evaluation meet band 3â4 descriptors: the conclusion is evaluated in context, methods are critiqued, and improvements are suggested, though not fully elaborated. The depth warrants an upperâband score of 4/6.

strengths: Comprehensive raw data table with summary statistics
Multiple well-labelled graphs (PM2.5 vs year, temperature vs year, scatter plot)
Correct calculation and reporting of Spearmanâs r and p-value
weaknesses: One graph uses an unintuitive scale and an unrelated line-fit image
Calculation workings contain formatting errors and unclear notation
P-value interpretation is ambiguous and an outlier year is not addressed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Results, Analysis and Conclusion align with band 3â4: data are presented appropriately and analysed correctly, and a valid conclusion is drawn. Errors and omissions in presentation and deeper analysis prevent a top-band award, so a 4/6 is justified.

strengths: Application proposal is justified by linking PM2.5 reduction measures to findings
Real-world examples from Tokyo, New York and Beijing strengthen relevance
Local policy context anchoring shows applied understanding
weaknesses: Evaluation of solution strengths and weaknesses lacks quantitative feasibility metrics
Justification remains general rather than tied directly to data outputs
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Applications correspond to band 3: a solution is justified based on study findings, and strengths/weaknesses are evaluated. While depth and quantitative metrics are limited, it meets the top descriptor for this criterion, so 3/3 is awarded.

strandSelections.reasoning: Environmental terminology (PM2.5, Urban Heat Island, Spearmanâs r) is used correctly, yet prose is verbose and occasionally repetitive.  This satisfies âeither uses appropriate terminology or is conciseâ; band 2, score 2.

The student links findings to health impacts, UHI, and policy implications, acknowledging correlation vs causation.  Evaluation notes limitations but does not systematically weigh evidence strength or quantify significance.  This corresponds to âevaluates â¦ with some omissionsâ; band 3-4, score 3.

The conclusion states a strong positive correlation and rejects the null, referencing the numerical r.  Caveats about other variables are mentioned, giving some validity.  Still, quantitative uncertainty (confidence limits) and depth of explanation are limited, so band 3-4, score 3.

Spearmanâs r and p-value are calculated, trends are noted, and mean/SD reported.  However, workings shown are disorganised, some formula lines are incorrect, and only bivariate correlation is exploredâno test of normality, no regression diagnostics.  Analysis is largely correct but incomplete, fitting band 3-4; score 3.

Improvements include adding more data sources, incorporating additional variables, and advanced modelling; these are meaningful and target key weaknesses but are not fully developed into actionable plans.  Hence band 3-4, score 3.

The method (collect secondary PM2.5 and temperature data, graph and run Spearman correlation) is repeatable and appropriate to answer a correlation question, but does not address data validity, homogeneity or potential confounders, so sufficiency is debatable.  This aligns with ârepeatable but insufficient for collecting *all* relevant dataâ; thus band 3-4, lower value 3.

Connections are explicitly made: PM2.5 absorbs sunlight, intensifies UHI, and consequently influences temperature; the research will test this correlation.  Links to urbanisation trends, policy interventions and potential wider applicability are also drawn.  The explanation is coherent, with only minor omissions (e.g., quantitative mechanism details).  This fits the âexplains comprehensivelyâ descriptor; band 5-6, score 5 is appropriate.

The student formulates a clear, single-sentence question that specifies the pollutant (PM2.5), the time-frame (2004-2022) and the response variable (annual mean temperature) for a defined location (Jakarta).  The scope is narrow enough for manageable secondary-data analysis and no ambiguities about what is to be investigated are apparent.  Minor wording issues (capitalisation, wordiness) do not detract from coherence, so a band 5-6 placement is warranted; a mid-band score of 5 reflects small stylistic weaknesses rather than conceptual ones.

Strengths (pollution reduction, health benefits) and weaknesses (cost, societal change time-lag) are identified but not evaluated in depth or quantitatively.  This matches band 2 (some relevant strengths/weaknesses evaluated).

The IA follows a standard ESS structure with headings and ToC, but formatting glitches, merged paragraphs, and occasional misplaced figures break sustained organisation.  Thus structure is present but not consistently maintained, consistent with band 2.

Strengths are only briefly implied (secondary data availability); weaknesses and limitations are listed in a table with suggested fixes.  Discussion is useful but not exhaustive (e.g., no comment on Spearman choice, data resolution).  Band 3-4, score 3 reflects this moderate depth.

The submission discusses air-pollution in Jakarta, outlines local PM2.5 exceedances, WHO limits, health impacts, and links to Urban Heat Island, then expands to global mortality figures.  These points provide solid context for why PM2.5 is environmentally significant.  While statistics are occasionally unsourced in-text, the description is detailed and relevant; hence the work meets the descriptor for a comprehensive discussion (band 5-6).  A score of 5 rather than 6 is assigned because quantitative context could be tighter and citations more explicit.

The student names âself-selected samplingâ and justifies it as suiting the research style, noting cross-checking of multiple sources.  The choice is described but not rigorously defended in statistical terms and does not address representativeness.  Descriptor for band 3-4 (âdescribes the choiceâ) is satisfied; score 3 reflects the modest depth.

Risks focus on academic honesty and calculation errors; ethical issues are limited to citation.  Physical or societal risks are irrelevant for secondary data, but the description lacks breadth (e.g., data privacy, misuse).  This is more than a list yet less than comprehensive, matching band 3-4; score 3.

A full table of raw data and three graphs are provided.  The scatter-plot directly addresses the relationship.  Axes are labelled, but one graph uses an unintuitive scale and the line-fit plot image appears unrelated to given values.  Some omission/errors are present; therefore band 3-4, score 3.

One main solution (Clean Air Action Plan plus supporting measures) is described with some justification linked to findings.  Rationale references PM2.5 reduction and urban heat mitigation, fulfilling descriptor for band 2 (âdescribes ... with weak justificationâ).
Overall argument flows, but long sentences, calculation fragments and displaced images impede reading in several sections.  Hence mainly logical but difficult to follow in partsâband 2, score 2.

annotations.comment: The environmental consequences section robustly ties findings to health impacts and policy challenges locally and regionally.
Figure references are inconsistent (refers to Figure 4 when discussing Figure 2); verify numbering to prevent reader confusion.
Real-life examples (Tokyo, New York, Beijing) effectively illustrate UHI solutions and broaden application relevance.
The limitations table correctly identifies key issues but lacks discussion of how biases quantitatively affect results; expand evaluation.
Suggested modifications (adding variables, new data sources) are meaningful; strengthen by detailing specific regression models and sources.
The aim statement concisely defines variables, location and timeframe, aligning clearly with the intended research focus.
The introductory paragraph combines background, context, and methodology in one long block. Break into focused subsections to enhance readability.
Self-selected sampling is not well suited to quantitative secondary data. Justify how this ensures representativeness and minimizes bias.
The rationale for choosing Spearman over Pearson is stated but lacks detail on data distribution tests; justify with normality assessment.
The calculation steps display formatting errors and unclear notation (e.g., misaligned \bar{x}); present clean, step-by-step workings.
The title clearly specifies the pollutant (PM2.5) and location (Jakarta), establishing immediate context for the investigation.
The research question is presented as a main heading, duplicating the title level. Consider demoting it to a subheading (H2) for consistent document structure.
The comprehensive Table of Contents reflects a well-planned structure and aids navigation through each section.
Page numbers in the TOC appear misaligned and inconsistent due to formatting glitches; verify layout to ensure clarity.
The research question is precise, referencing PM2.5, timeframe, and mean temperature, providing a focused investigable query.
The connection to Urban Heat Island is introduced but the paragraph ends abruptly. Complete the explanation and include citations for the mechanism.
The local context paragraph quantifies PM2.5 exceedance with WHO limits, strengthening environmental relevance.
The global context effectively references mortality figures and policy implications, demonstrating wide significance.
The map of Jakarta enriches geographical context and aids reader orientation within the study area.
Background statistics lack clear in-text citations; include source references directly after numerical values to strengthen credibility.
The hypothesis anticipates a strong positive correlation but could be tightened and phrased directionally for clarity.
Scientific justification cites Busen et al. without page numbers or publication details; provide full reference information.
The variables table is overcrowded and overlaps text; reformat to clearly delineate independent, dependent, and controlled variables.
Controlled variables are well defined with data source justification, promoting consistency across the dataset.
The uncontrolled variables section names CO2 and weather but does not quantify their potential effects; elaborate on their likely impact.
Equipment table clearly lists apparatus and purposes, supporting repeatability of data collection methods.
Procedure step referencing an âonline calculatorâ lacks detail on formula or software. Specify tools and formula to ensure repeatability.
The justification section ends mid-sentence; complete the rationale linking sampling choice back to research objectives.
Ethical considerations include citation practices and data triangulation, demonstrating awareness of bias mitigation.
Risk assessment focuses on academic integrity but omits data licensing or privacy considerations; expand to cover all secondary-data risks.
The raw data table is comprehensive and includes summary statistics, ensuring transparency in data presentation.
An anomalous PM2.5 drop in 2017 (29.7 Âµg/mÂ³) is not addressed in the presentation; annotate or explain this outlier in results.
The PM2.5âYear plot effectively reveals pollution trends over time, aiding visual interpretation.
The temperature vs. year graph clearly shows stability of the variable, complementing the PM2.5 analysis.
Discussion of mean and standard deviation would benefit from inclusion of confidence intervals to quantify uncertainty.
The scatter plot aids visual assessment of correlation strength between PM2.5 and temperature.
Summation formulas appear truncated and inconsistent; verify each term and ensure mathematical clarity in the presentation.
Present the final Spearman formula succinctly as $$r_s = \frac{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2 \sum(y_i-\bar{y})^2}}$$ to improve clarity.
P-value interpretation is unclear (âP(x â¤ 6.5173) = 1â); clarify test statistic, degrees of freedom, and significance level.
The discussion aptly links the high correlation to the hypothesis and acknowledges limitations on causation.
The conclusion reiterates the strong correlation with explicit $$r=0.8451$$, reinforcing the data-driven outcome.
The conclusion mixes findings with recommendations; separate pure conclusion from actionable recommendations to improve coherence.
Recommendations would be stronger if tied quantitatively to study findings (e.g., predicted PM2.5 reduction and temperature response).
The applications section anchors solutions in local policy context, demonstrating applied understanding.
Solution evaluation identifies socio-economic barriers but lacks feasibility metrics (cost, implementation timeline); include concrete assessments.
Reference list includes diverse authoritative sources (WeatherSpark, NASA), enhancing data validity.
References are inconsistently formatted and missing key elements; adopt a consistent citation style (e.g., APA) for all entries.
